items.question: Who uses hypothesis testing in ML and Gen AI?
items.answer: Data scientists running A/B tests, researchers comparing model benchmarks, and engineers validating latency or quality changes—all need formal rules for when a observed difference is more than sampling noise.

items.question: What is a p-value?
items.answer: The probability, assuming H₀ is true, of obtaining a test statistic at least as extreme as the one observed. It measures how surprising the data are under the null—not the probability that H₀ is true.

items.question: When do you reject the null hypothesis?
items.answer: When the p-value is less than or equal to the pre-specified significance level α (e.g. 0.05), meaning the data are sufficiently unlikely under H₀ to abandon the default claim.

items.question: Where does the t-distribution appear?
items.answer: In one- and two-sample t-tests when population variance is unknown and estimated from the sample; the test statistic is compared to a t-distribution with n−1 (or Welch) degrees of freedom.

items.question: Why pre-specify α before collecting data?
items.answer: To control the Type I error rate and avoid post-hoc moving of the goalposts. Changing α after seeing the p-value inflates false-positive risk and breaks the intended error guarantees.

items.question: How is a one-sample t statistic computed?
items.answer: t = (x̄ − μ₀) / (s/√n): sample mean minus null mean, divided by the standard error of the mean. The result is compared to the t-distribution to obtain a p-value.

items.question: p-value vs confidence interval: what does each emphasize?
items.answer: A p-value answers whether evidence exceeds a threshold against H₀; it does not quantify effect size. A confidence interval gives a range of plausible parameter values and shows magnitude plus uncertainty—often more informative for decisions.

items.question: One-sided vs two-sided tests: when does the choice matter?
items.answer: A one-sided test asks if an effect is strictly in one direction (e.g. μ < μ₀) and puts all α in that tail—more power for that direction but cannot detect effects in the opposite direction. A two-sided test splits α across both tails and is the default when direction is not fixed in advance.

items.question: Cause: you lower α from 0.05 to 0.01 without increasing sample size. Effect?
items.answer: Type I error rate decreases (fewer false positives), but Type II error rate β typically increases and statistical power drops—you need stronger evidence to reject H₀, so real effects are easier to miss.

items.question: Cause: sample size n increases while true effect stays fixed. Effect?
items.answer: Standard error s/√n shrinks, |t| grows, p-values tend to decrease, and power to detect the real effect increases—small but genuine differences become test-detectable.
